segments.title: Target Tirpitz and the Arctic convoy threat
segments.summary: Patrick Bishop's Target Tirpitz introduces the combined air, submarine, and surface-raider danger that made the Arctic convoys exceptionally difficult.
segments.body: The book discussion places attacks on Tirpitz within the wider problem of sustaining Arctic convoys to the Soviet Union. Clarke describes the threat as a three-way balance among Luftwaffe aircraft operating from northern Norway, U-boats, and major German surface raiders. The losses and warnings surrounding PQ 15, PQ 16, and especially PQ 17 show why summer daylight operations were so dangerous and why planners expected a convoy disaster.

evidence.note: Clarke reads and discusses Target Tirpitz, convoy losses, northern Norwegian airfields, and the combined U-boat, aircraft, and surface-raider threat.

segments.title: How the Long Range Desert Group raided
segments.summary: John Laffin's Raiders illustrates how range, supply discipline, concealment, captured-vehicle ambiguity, and surprise made LRDG attacks possible.
segments.body: The selected account emphasizes the logistical foundation of desert raiding: patrols could cover roughly two thousand miles and sometimes needed many trucks simply to carry fuel, water, ammunition, and supplies. It follows an LRDG patrol that survived air attack, hid in a familiar wadi, reconnoitred Axis traffic, and approached a roadside target in trucks that attracted little notice because German and Italian forces also used captured British vehicles. After a sudden machine-gun and grenade attack, the patrol withdrew, concealed itself again, and returned without casualties. Clarke connects this combination of mobility, support, and opportunism to later special-forces support methods.

evidence.note: The reading explains desert patrol range, fuel and water requirements, forward bases, and LRDG raiding activity.
A patrol uses reconnaissance, disguise by familiarity, concentrated fire, withdrawal, and concealment to attack Axis road traffic without casualties.

segments.title: Glider lessons behind the Pegasus Bridge assault
segments.summary: Stephen Ambrose's Pegasus Bridge links the famous D-Day coup de main to hard glider experience and lessons learned during the invasion of Sicily.
segments.body: The reading follows men on both sides as they trained before the Normandy invasion, then concentrates on British glider-pilot experience with American Waco aircraft. During the Sicily landings, many tow aircraft released their gliders too far offshore, drowning troops before they could reach the fight. One pilot repeatedly refused to release until closer to land, survived a rough landing near an Italian position, and later returned to Britain for Operation Deadstick. The episode explains why John Howard increased swimming requirements and shows how operational lessons shaped preparation for Pegasus Bridge.

evidence.note: The passage introduces German and Allied personnel training before the bridge operation.
The reading recounts Waco glider training, disastrous offshore releases at Sicily, a pilot's landing, and his later participation in Operation Deadstick.

segments.title: Operation Chariot's dockyard assault teams
segments.summary: C. E. Lucas Phillips's account of the St Nazaire Raid follows a commando team clearing and demolishing German gun positions beside the dock.
segments.body: The final selection turns to the St Nazaire Raid, also known as Operation Chariot. A commando team moves through the dockyard using knowledge learned from a model, carrying scaling ladders and helping a wounded sergeant. After seeing German troops flee and overcoming a jammed weapon, the attackers use grenades to clear two gun positions on the pumping-station roof and demolish them. The short extract highlights the rehearsed, task-specific action that accompanied the raid's better-known use of a destroyer against the dry dock.

evidence.note: Clarke reads an account of the St Nazaire assault team locating, clearing, and demolishing dockyard gun positions.
